sentences.text: Well, yes.
I must have been around eight, eight or nine.
The war started when I was six.
And this-- Plaszow was in 1943.
That's right.
No.
Just being there.
Oh, sure.
Good lord, yes-- not only from then, but he was with us after the war in Argentina practically [INAUDIBLE] well.
Well, I-- you must remember that I was very small.
I couldn't really judge a man.
He was very good looking.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember him practically always smiling.
And especially, he didn't look like anyone one should-- one could be scared of.
No.
No.
I was too small.
I assume that in some papers, it said that I did something.
But as far, as far as I remember at least I never did anything.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, as normal as the circumstances would allow.
I don't think, you know, it was a questions of games, say, of loud games, or running around.
Perhaps we did.
I don't, I don't remember.
But yes, we, we did play.
Yes.
Yes.
They worked in the factory.
But what they did, I haven't got the faintest idea.
Because I don't think, I don't think either of them knew anything about, about enamel works.
I don't think so.
I think I was mainly in the barracks or--
You're asking specifically what were the barracks like there?
I think there were more beds than shelves there.
The barracks [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were mainly to separate men and women and to get in as many people in every place possible.
So the beds must have been more or less the same as everywhere, as in all the other camps.
Well, in the camp with my mother and father but in the barracks with my mother.
I think everybody, everybody was-- you sort of lived with the idea that either this was a bad dream or that you might not survive the day or the next day.
And it of dif-- it, it wasn't [? war. ?] It wasn't very, you know, apt to, to make plans for anything.
We just survived the day.
And the next day took care of itself.
That's right.
And then I don't know what happened.
But they sent a part of the people who-- at least, I was sent back to, to Plaszow.
I think with my mother, not my father.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But weren't sent to Plaszow for a long time, because as I said, Plaszow was being liquidated.
You know, as the war went on, more and more places were closed down.
And pe-- the people were sent to others.
And we were sent to a Birkenau [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through-- we stopped on the way, I think, in two, or three, or four small camps.
But I don't remember what they were like.
And I know they were sort of like two days' stay over.
Because I don't-- the rest of the time was spent in the train.
Very, very nasty.
Well [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] it was almost winter.
And those-- the, the trains were animal transport things.
There were no facilities for anything inside-- no bathroom, not even a-- I don't think they, they even put in a-- sorry.
A bucket, bucket.
And then it was all mixed, women, men, and very, very full.
You could, you could stand up.
But you couldn't sit down.
And no water and no food, and it was quite a long trip.
I don't remember.
I remember that we arrived in Birkenau in the middle of the night.
But I don't remember how long the trip took-- Several days.
Well, I think that is when they-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was going back.
Can I go back?
I was going back to Plaszow and that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when they sent the children away.
Because I-- that's another thing I do remember because of one of those happenings.
One morning shortly before Plaszow closed down, we were called as usual at 6:00 in the morning to the appellplatz.
But it was a little different, because-- and it was-- we saw it, because we were surrounded by Germans with machine-- soldiers with machine guns.
And you know, I must say that the Germans had a kind of-- I know they must have, have had very good publicity professionals.
Because they always found the right song for the right moment.
Like when they were taking the children away, you know the appellplatz had all loudspeakers all around.
And they were all playing a song called, [GERMAN SPEECH].
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That is another thing I remember fairl-- quite clearly.
Goodnight Mother, Goodnight?
Right.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And well, only 13 of us saved ourselves.
And I mean exactly that.
Some were hidden by their parents who knew about what was going to happen before.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't remember very well.
But I was told by, by my mother who was there that I went down one of the latrines, one of the holes, and hid there.
I don't remember whether, whether this did happen or not.
But this is what my mother said I did.
So I assume she knows.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wanted to go back to that.
Because that was one of the terrorific things that happened.
And I guess that is when the real, the real fear started.
And then I guess it never went away until the end of the war.
It was-- it wasn't throughout the war-- I mean, not the first part.
Not-- perhaps not until that moment.
But from that moment on, it did.
Go on.
Well, and then as I said, we were sent to Birkenau.
And there again was the-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] first of all, they took us to, to those showers.
And at least they, they shaved all my hair off.
I don't know what happened to, to most of the people.
My mother didn't recognize me, recognize me when she, when she saw me.
As a matter of fact, she. she asked-- and that, that is another thing I remember.
She said, little boy, did you ever-- did you see my, my daughter?
And they-- then they gave us those ridiculous clothes.
Because see, every transport that came came with their-- very many didn't come-- even if they came from the camps, they had some clothes.
And very many came from-- not from Poland.
They came directly from Romania, from Bulgaria, et cetera.
And they came with, with their clothes, with their suitcases.
And they were all taken away.
And most of them went to the ovens.
But the clothe were given to, to the newcomers, like summer clothes in the middle of the winter.
And then they started the segregation thing-- this right, left, right, left.
I was very big for my age.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And somehow, I got to segregated into the right direction.
It wouldn't have mattered too much, because there was already an order that the 300 people who were from Schindler's weren't to be touched.
But we didn't know about it.
And, and it was terrifying.
We weren't in Birkenau very long.
I think we were there for two or three weeks.
I guess.
I remember being with, with my mother right at the beginning when we came to Birkenau and then through the bath routine, et cetera, shower.
But I don't remember-- and then I remember being with her when-- on the train going to Birkenau-- to, going to Brunnlitz.
But I don't remember being with her in, in Birkenau.
And I don't, for instance-- I never knew why she has the number and my father had that.
She had the number, and so did my father.
But I never did.
So somebody must, must have hidden me somewhere.
Oh.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Just being scared, just-- no, you, you can't really explain it.
It's-- it's like knowing you're going to-- knowing you're dead, just waiting for the moment when, when you die.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It's ver-- it's terrifying.
It real-- that part really is.
Then we were very close to the, to the ovens.
And the smell is-- of course, you don't know to start off with what the smell is.
But somebody-- there is always somebody who very quick, quickly explains.
Yes.
Somebody told me.
I must have asked and--
Oh yes, I think.
I think by the time, I believed, believed anything that, that was happening.
Because in the beginning, well, I was very little.
And, I can't ima-- even-- children, children can't imagine certain things.
You know, children can't imagine dying.
Something will happen and death will go way.
But when you're 10 or 11 and you've lived through years of this, well, you know it-- it's there.
It exists.
And since you were a child, you were the most likely to, to go.
We knew what was happening in the gas-- in the gas, gas chambers.
What would people talk about in a concentration camp?
Especially Birkenau, which was not a working camp-- it was the death camp.
The pre-- so everybody talked about nothing else but that.
And you couldn't, you couldn't help listening or hearing.
And it wasn't any longer a question of believing.
Because there it was.
And there you smelt it.
And there you saw it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I suppose I was scared, yes.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No.
I guess it was more of the same.
Because you just were there.
And whenever they called you to go out, you thought-- all you thought was, well, here it comes.
And when you're a child, you can't even rationalize it.
Because when you're, when you're older, you can rationalize death.
But when you're a child, you can't.
It's something absolutely terrifying.
And you can't even say, well, everybody dies eventually.
So sooner or later-- well, you'll miss some nice things.
You'll miss some bad things.
But eventually, you're dead.
You can't.
When you're a child, you don't think that way.
You just think, this is terrible.
This is going to hurt.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well I guess--
No, not in Birkenau-- there weren't.
Legally, there were no children in Bir-- in Birkenau.
There may have been-- I mean, the children who later went to Brunnlitz, to Schindler's last camp.
There were, I don't know, 10 or 15 of u, whoever was on his list.
They were somewhere around in, in Birkenau.
But nobody ever saw them.
No, no.
You couldn't be seen there if you were a child.
You wouldn't last a second.
In the barracks, mainly in the barracks, and hidden in some corner or hidden in a bed made up in a, in a-- not a bed because weren't any beds-- just hidden, just sort of nonexistent.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And then came the great news that we were going to Czechoslovakia, to, to Schindler's.
Yes, I think, I think that my mother came and told me.
Because my mother was very worried. i grandmother had been separated in the wrong direction.
And my mother couldn't find her.
She wasn't in any of the barracks in Birken-- Birkenau.
But when they got us together to go to Brunnlitz, she was there, so--
That's right.
By train.
No.
It's another blank.
I don't know why those are blanking, because some very-- some really terrorific things I remember perfectly.
And the train ride shouldn't have been that terrorific.
And I don't remember it at all.
No.
It must have been winter, because we arrived in Birkenau in winter.
We were always cold.
Because the clothes we were given was summer-- I was given summer clothes.
And there was never enough food.
Sometimes, there just wasn't any food.
And there very often wasn't any water on the, on the trip.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But one survived.
That, that was the whole, the whole purpose of the, of the exercise, to survive.
There were barracks.
There was also SS, although their role wasn't as important there.
Because it was-- Brunnlitz was a munitions factory.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] You lived better.
The food was much worse, because that was already '45, '44-- end of '44, beginning of '45.
And the Germans were losing the war.
And there wasn't enough food for them, least of all for us.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was that-- it wasn't that bad.
I remember I used to sing.
I used to have a very good voice, singing voice.
And I used to sing every evening for the whole barrack, our barrack.
Songs.
Songs I learned as a child, songs I learned from the maids, romantic songs, whatever.
No.
No, I don't remember any Polish songs.
You see, [?
Carol, ?] the thing is that I have for years, and years, and years, whenever-- when I, when I was about 15, I decided that to be able to live some kind of a life, I had to forget all that.
And then about 30 or 40 years later, I decided, well, it's ridiculous.
You can't just erase a whole part of your life.
The things is that I did, when I decided to do it, I did erase about 40% of everything that happened.
And I couldn't get it back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that's where the-- why wouldn't I remember the names of the songs?
I just don't.
It was one of the things that must have touched me in some way.
And I didn't want to remember it.
But you do remember singing for the barracks?
Yes, but I do remember singing.
I sang in Polish.
Oh, the people loved it.
It was [? some ?] entertainment in a place where there was no entertainment.
And everybody-- there were other people who did something, something funny or somebody else who sang in the evening just to fill up time.

You couldn't-- you know, even if you live scared and even if you don't have enough food, you still need something more than just waking up, and going to sleep, and going to work, and waking up, and going to sleep.
Because otherwise, people wouldn't care whether they sur-- whether they survived or not.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, and then the rest of it is more, more or less heresay, really.
Because I told you, my father was sent to Gross-Rosen.
He wasn't-- he was with us in Brunnlitz ever.
But that's only an aside.
We heard that the Russians were very close and the Germans were leaving.
But apparently, a short time before, the SS commander told Schindler that he wasn't going to leave any Jew alive.
And he was going to take us all to the woods there and have us shot.
And I know that Oskar-- I don't know what Oskar did to persuade him not to.
But the last memory I have of the Germans was they were running away.
They were leaving.
But they were still shooting at us.
And some people got killed.
And then the Russians came.
And I guess that was the end of the war from, for us.
I baked a cake for his birthday once.
I can't, because I can't even imagine where I could have baked it.
Somebody must have taken me to the main kitchens.
Yes.
But it's a very vague memory.
I haven't got the vaguest idea.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some-- you see, some of it is hearsay, things in my mother told me I did.
So I'm not quite sure whether I remember baking it or whether I remember my mother telling me that I baked it.
No.
No, which is why I say they are hearsay memories.
Oh, yes, yes-- Him and her.
Yes.
I don't know.
She's nuts.
She lives in Argentina.
